01a_quotes.title: Generative AI's Impact on Graduate Student Writing Productivity and Quality

study_design: Employing a mixed-methods design, we compared students' performance on professional writing tasks conducted without and with genAI assistance.
In this study, we employed a mixed-methods approach to examine the effects of genAI assistance on a graded writing assignment.
All participants completed three writing assignments in the order of no genAI, full genAI, and genAI-assisted.
A 2x2 mixed-model ANOVA tested for the difference in this writing time over writing condition (no genAI, genAI-assisted) and English language status (native English speaker, ESL).
A 2x2 mixed-model ANOVA tested for the difference in writing performance across writing conditions (no genAI, genAI-assisted) and English language status (native English speaker, ESL).
A 2x2 mixed-model ANOVA tested for the difference in perceived writing competency over time (pre, post) and English language status (native English speaker, ESL).

While the present study did involve a comparison condition in which students completed their writing assignment without genAI assistance, due to logistical and pedagogical constraints we were not able to counterbalance the order of writing conditions.
task: A sequence of professional memo writing exercises was designed to consistently involve the same five core professional research and writing sub-tasks of summarization of a news article, web research to find survey results, analysis and synthesis of different survey results, policy development, and critical analysis.

The assignment prompt was written as if from a different stakeholder (e.g., the CEO), highlights an observation about AI based on a research study or headline, shares two results from the source, requests a deliverable that summarizes the cited source, calls on the student to do additional research to assess the representativeness of the results, requires them to explain the risks and benefits of the generative AI in the particular context, and finally to present a well-argued policy for the specific work context (see Supplementary Material 1 for a sample assignment).
Participants were randomly assigned writing assignment version A, B, or C to complete the first week of classes.
This first assignment represented the no genAI condition.
The second assignment was the full genAI condition.
After this instruction, students were given their third writing assignment.
Students could choose whether to complete this assignment with genAI assistance or independently without genAI.
population: Participants were 28 graduate students enrolled in the graduate course toward a degree program during the spring 2024 term.
The course was a 7-week graduate elective class at Carnegie Mellon University.
One student opted out of the study, resulting in a final sample size of 27 participants.
Participants were from a variety of academic disciplines including information systems and public policy (63%), business (25.9%), computer science (7.4%), and humanities (3.7%).
One participant was enrolled in a doctoral program and all remaining participants were in Master's programs.
Fifteen participants (55.6%) self-reported being English as a Second Language (ESL) student while 12 participants (44.4%) were native English speakers.
Eleven participants (40.7%) self-reported having an extensive technical background in computer science or programming.
Ten participants self-reported having no technical background and six reported having a little background.
Twenty-three (85.2%) participants reported having prior experience using ChatGPT or other AI to help with their coursework.
arms: Participants completed the pre survey immediately before the start of the course.
Participants were randomly assigned writing assignment version A, B, or C to complete the first week of classes.
This first assignment represented the no genAI condition.
The second assignment was the full genAI condition.
This was an in-class assignment during the second week of the course and participants were only permitted 30 minutes to complete it.
This condition is not part of the comparison of this study as it primarily served as an instructional tool.
After the first assignment but before the third, the course instructor provided several hours of take-home reading and in-class instruction on how to effectively co-author with genAI.
After this instruction, students were given their third writing assignment.
Students could choose whether to complete this assignment with genAI assistance or independently without genAI.
Every student chose to use genAI as an assistant and so this third assignment represents our genAI-assisted condition.
outcome_definitions: Students were asked to keep track of how long they were actively engaged in completing their memo writing assignments (both research and writing) and to submit this time along with their deliverable.
Writing performance was defined by students' earned grades on a standardized grading rubric (see Supplementary Material 2).
All rubric criteria were scored on a scale of 1 (insufficient), 2 (sufficient), or 3 (proficient).
Quality of summarization was a single criterion.
External survey findings involved two criteria of quality of external survey research as well as integration and analysis of survey results.
Policy recommendation involved three criteria of articulation of their policy, how logical and comprehensive their policy was, and how well they argued and supported their policy with analysis.
Writing style had two criteria of style, flow, and concision as well as organization.
The maximum grade on each memo assignment was 24 points.

Students self-reported whether or not they are a native English speaker, their graduate program enrollment, technical background in computer science or programming (none, a little, extensive), whether or not they had previously used ChatGPT or other AI to help with coursework (yes, no), and how they self-evaluate their professional English writing competency compared to other graduate students at CMU (1 = worse than almost all graduate students at CMU, 5 = Better than almost all graduate students at CMU).
Participants took a post survey to determine how students used the genAI tool in the course and their views about the tool.

They self-reported on which research and writing sub-tasks they used AI (summarization, web research, policy development, analysis, writing, or none of the above), how useful the AI tool was in each of the above tasks (1 = not useful, 4 = very useful), how much they believe their research and writing quality improved from the first to the third assignment (1 = did not improve, 5 = significantly improved), how much they believe their research and writing speed improved from the first to the third assignment (1 = did not improve, 5 = significantly improved), how much they attribute any improvement in writing quality and/or speed to the use of AI versus experience gained in completing similar assignments (1 = 100% from completing similar assignments, 5 = 50/50 improvement from experience with a similar assignment and benefits from using AI, 10 = 100% from using the AI tool), and how much they value the benefits of AI tools to their writing quality versus speed (1 = 100% value the quality, 5 = equally value quality/speed improvement, 10 =100% value the speed).
statistical_results: Results showed genAI, with proper instruction, reduced writing time by 64.5% and improved writing quality from a B+ to an A.

There was a significant main effect of writing condition on writing time (f(1,23) = 23.15, p < .001, ηp2 = .50) showing that students spent significantly less time writing their memo when they used genAI assistance.
On average, genAI assistance reduced the time spent completing their policy memo assignment by 64.5%.

There was no main effect of English language status (f(1,23) = 1.09, p = .31, ηp2 = .05) meaning native English speakers and ESL students spent comparable time preparing their memo writing assignments.

There was no interaction between writing condition and English language status on writing time (f(1,23) = .45, p = .51, ηp2 = .02) meaning, statistically, the amount of time saved writing with genAI assistance did not differ between native English speakers and ESL students (see Figure 2).
There was a significant main effect of writing condition (f(1,25) = 4.74, p = .04, ηp2 = .16) indicating that students earned higher grades on the assignment prepared with genAI assistance.
On average, students' grades change from a B+ (M = 88.3%, SD = 10.3%) without genAI to an A (M = 94.1%, SD = 8.0%) with genAI assistance.
There was no main effect of English language status (f(1,25) = .35, p = .56, ηp2 = .01) meaning ESL students and native English speakers earned equivalent assignment grades.

There was no interaction of writing condition and English language status (f(1,25) = .12, p = .74, ηp2 = .01) meaning the amount of change in assignment grades did not differ between native English speakers and ESL students (see Figure 3).
Organization: f(1,26) = 3.96, p = .06, ηp2 = .13
Style, flow, and concision: f(1,26) = 3.25, p = .08, ηp2 = .11
External survey research: f(1,26) = 2.36, p = .14, ηp2 = .08
Summarization: f(1,26) = 2.08, p = .16, ηp2 = .07
External survey analysis: f(1,26) = 1.41, p = .25, ηp2 = .05
Policy argument: f(1,26) = 1.15, p = .29, ηp2 = .04
Policy articulation: f(1,26) = .68, p = .42, ηp2 = .03
Policy logic and holistics: f(1,26) = .06, p = .81, ηp2 = .00

There was a significant effect of English language status on perceived writing competency (f(1,25) = 8.70, p = .007, ηp2 = .26) with ESL students reporting lower perceived competency than native English speakers.
There was no main effect of time (f(1,25) = 1.44, p = .24, ηp2 = .05) indicating that students' perceived writing competency did not significantly change from the beginning to the end of the course.
There was, however, a marginally significant interaction between English language status and time (f(1,25) = 3.99, p = .06, ηp2 = .14, see Figure 4).

Follow-up pairwise testing found that coming into the course, ESL students self-evaluated their English writing competency significantly lower than did native English speakers (t(25) = 3.49, p = .002, d = 1.35).

By the end of the semester, this difference had disappeared (t(25) = 1.07, p = .30, d = .41) with ESL students reporting equivalent perceived writing competency to their native English speaking peers.

Additionally, ESL students showed a marginally significant increase in their perceived writing competency over time (t(14) = -2.09, p = .06, d = .54) whereas native English speaking students did not change in their perceived competency (t(11) = .69, p = .50, d = .20).
Students found the genAI's assistance to be the most useful for summarization (M = 3.52 (max = 4), SD = .75) and assistance in writing (M = 3.50, SD = .71).
The tool's usefulness for the sub-tasks of web research (M = 3.08, SD = .81), policy development (M = 3.04, SD = .82), and analysis (M = 2.87, SD = .92) was a bit lower.
ESL students found the genAI tool to be significantly more useful in the writing process than did native English speaking students (t(24) = -2.09, p = .047).
They reported feeling their speed had improved considerably (M = 4.44 (max = 5), SD = 1.01).
Native English speaking students reported feeling more significant improvement in their speed than did ESL students (t(15.92) = 2.60, p = .02).
Students attributed their improvement in writing productivity and quality more to GenAI (66.7% weight) than repeated practice with assignments.
They reported feeling like their quality had somewhat improved (M = 3.74 (max = 5), SD = .76).
This sense of improvement in quality was slightly higher in ESL than native English speaking students though this failed to reach statistical significance (t(25) = -.96, p = .35).

This perception of quality significantly correlated with the change in total assignment grade from assignment 1 (no genAI) to assignment 3 (genAI-assisted) (r(27) = .45, p = .02, R2 = .20) such that students who perceived a greater improvement in their writing quality showed a larger increase in their grade.

We find that genAI assistance following this instruction significantly improved writing productivity, reducing the time spent on research and writing tasks by 64.5%, from a mean of 191.8 minutes (SD = 130.3 minutes) on the no-genAI assignment to a mean of 68.1 minutes (SD = 28.4 minutes) on the genAI-assisted assignment.
GenAI use also significantly boosted quality, as average students' grades improved from a B+ (M = 88.3%, SD = 10.3%) without genAI to an A (M = 94.1%, SD = 8.0%) with genAI assistance.

When asked "How do you value the benefits of AI tools to your research/writing quality versus speed?" with response anchors of 1 = 100% value quality, 5 = equally value quality and speed, and 10 = 100% value speed, the average was 6.7.

When asked how much they thought their research and writing speed improved on the genAI-assisted memo assignment, they reported feeling like their speed had considerably improved (M = 4.44 (max = 5), SD = 1.01).
When asked the same question about research and writing quality, they reported a weaker sense of improvement (M = 3.74 (max = 5), SD = .76).

When asked "How much do you attribute the improvement in your writing quality and/or speed to the use of AI versus your gained experience in completing similar assignments?" on average, students attributed the change more to genAI than repeated practice (67% due to genAI).

In productivity, ESL students spent on average 212.36 minutes (SD = 116.75 min) preparing their first assignment without genAI, while native English speaking students spent on average 165.64 minutes (SD = 147.32 min).

With genAI assistance, ESL students closed the time to completion gap, averaging only 10 minutes longer to complete the assignment compared to native English speakers, down from an average 47 minute gap on the first assignment.
The observed data show ESL students had an 85% average grade on the no-genAI assignment compared to 87% for native English speaking students.
In the final assignment with genAI assistance, cohorts earned equivalent grades (MNative = 94.4%, MESL = 93.9%).
On the survey taken at the beginning of the course, ESL students self-evaluated their English writing competency significantly lower than native English speakers (p = .002).
By the end of the course, this difference had disappeared (p = .30), with ESL students reporting equivalent perceived writing competency to their native English speaking peers.
ESL students additionally found the genAI tool to be significantly more useful than did native English speaking students (p = .047).
Out of the 29 students in the study, many students reported using the tool for writing sub-tasks, specifically summarization (23 students), writing (22 students), and/or web research (22 students).

Additionally, 18 students reported using the tool for policy development (e.g., articulating the workplace policy in the assignment scenario) and 18 students used it for analysis (e.g., of survey results, policies, arguments).
On average, students used the genAI tool for assistance in 3.8 of these 5 sub-task areas.
All students reported they intended to use the tool in future writing tasks.

Also, a majority of respondents (16 students, 59.26%) believed that "a class that teaches AI assisted productivity, such as AI-enabled research and writing, should be required to receive a graduate degree from CMU."
ambiguity_or_caveats: While the present study did involve a comparison condition in which students completed their writing assignment without genAI assistance, due to logistical and pedagogical constraints we were not able to counterbalance the order of writing conditions.
While we found significant effects on student performance (both writing speed and quality), we cannot definitively attribute that impact to genAI use alone.

It is possible that other factors could have (partially) contributed to improvements in performance, such as repeated practice with the assignment, though when asked, students attributed their improvement more to the genAI assistance than practice.
Future studies could consider replicating in a controlled lab setting in which the order of conditions can be randomized to strengthen the causal inference.
Additionally, because this was a real classroom, the sample of the present study was constrained by the type and number of students enrolled.
This resulted in a self-selected group of students interested in taking an elective course on AI, 85.2% of whom reported having used genAI to help with their previous course work.

This was a limit on the power of this study to detect all of the possible effects of genAI assistance and disaggregated analyses testing whether there are differential effects for students with different English language backgrounds.
For instance, while not statistically significant, our ESL students took longer to complete their first writing assignments and scored lower than native English speakers.
Both speed and quality were much more similar when students used genAI assistance.
This pattern and the other results of the present study are suggestive that writing with genAI assistance could serve as an equity tool to support all students in their research and writing process.
Another limitation is that the grading rubric was implemented by the professor, who was not blinded to the condition, which could impact the validity of the quality assessments.
However, this is mitigated by a detailed grading rubric.
However, the trend in the data suggests that ESL students showed slightly greater improvements in productivity, though this difference was not statistically significant.
However, the trend in the data suggests that ESL students showed slightly greater improvements in performance, though this difference was not statistically significant.
The reported p-values are uncorrected for multiple comparisons.

01a_quotes.notes: This is an empirical study with a mixed-methods design comparing writing performance with and without genAI assistance among graduate students. The study includes both quantitative measures (writing time, grades on standardized rubric) and qualitative data (student perceptions via surveys and open-ended responses). The primary comparison is between a no-genAI condition (Assignment 1) and a genAI-assisted condition (Assignment 3), with a full-genAI condition (Assignment 2) serving as an instructional tool rather than a data source. The study also examines differential effects by English language background (ESL vs. native English speakers). Key findings include a 64.5% reduction in writing time and improvement from B+ to A grades with genAI assistance, with observed (though not statistically significant) leveling effects for ESL students.

01_extraction.title: Generative AI's Impact on Graduate Student Writing Productivity and Quality
01_extraction.study_design_description: A mixed-methods study comparing students' performance on professional writing tasks conducted without and with genAI assistance. All participants completed three writing assignments in the order of no genAI, full genAI, and genAI-assisted. The first assignment represented the no genAI condition, the second was the full genAI condition (serving as an instructional tool), and the third was the genAI-assisted condition. Due to logistical and pedagogical constraints, the order of writing conditions was not counterbalanced. A 2×2 mixed-model ANOVA tested for differences in writing time, writing performance, and perceived writing competency across writing conditions and English language status.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: A sequence of professional memo writing exercises involving five core professional research and writing sub-tasks: summarization of a news article, web research to find survey results, analysis and synthesis of different survey results, policy development, and critical analysis. The assignment prompt was written as if from a different stakeholder (e.g., the CEO), highlighted an observation about AI based on a research study or headline, shared two results from the source, requested a deliverable that summarizes the cited source, called on the student to do additional research to assess the representativeness of the results, required them to explain the risks and benefits of the generative AI in the particular context, and finally to present a well-argued policy for the specific work context.
01_extraction.population_description: Participants were 27 graduate students (one student opted out of the initial 28) enrolled in a 7-week graduate elective class at Carnegie Mellon University during the spring 2024 term. Participants were from a variety of academic disciplines including information systems and public policy (63%), business (25.9%), computer science (7.4%), and humanities (3.7%). One participant was enrolled in a doctoral program and all remaining participants were in Master's programs. Fifteen participants (55.6%) self-reported being English as a Second Language (ESL) student while 12 participants (44.4%) were native English speakers. Eleven participants (40.7%) self-reported having an extensive technical background in computer science or programming. Twenty-three (85.2%) participants reported having prior experience using ChatGPT or other AI to help with their coursework.

arms.description: First assignment completed without genAI assistance. Participants were randomly assigned writing assignment version A, B, or C to complete the first week of classes.

Third writing assignment completed with genAI assistance. After instruction on how to effectively co-author with genAI, students were given their third writing assignment and could choose whether to complete it with genAI assistance or independently without genAI. Every student chose to use genAI as an assistant.

outcomes.outcome_description: Students were asked to keep track of how long they were actively engaged in completing their memo writing assignments (both research and writing) and to submit this time along with their deliverable.

Writing performance was defined by students' earned grades on a standardized grading rubric. All rubric criteria were scored on a scale of 1 (insufficient), 2 (sufficient), or 3 (proficient). The maximum grade on each memo assignment was 24 points.
Quality of summarization was a single criterion on the grading rubric, scored on a scale of 1 (insufficient), 2 (sufficient), or 3 (proficient).

External survey findings involved two criteria of quality of external survey research as well as integration and analysis of survey results, scored on a scale of 1 (insufficient), 2 (sufficient), or 3 (proficient).
Analysis of survey results, one of two criteria under external survey findings, scored on a scale of 1 (insufficient), 2 (sufficient), or 3 (proficient).

Policy recommendation involved three criteria of articulation of their policy, how logical and comprehensive their policy was, and how well they argued and supported their policy with analysis. Articulation of policy was scored on a scale of 1 (insufficient), 2 (sufficient), or 3 (proficient).
How logical and comprehensive the policy was, one of three criteria under policy recommendation, scored on a scale of 1 (insufficient), 2 (sufficient), or 3 (proficient).
How well students argued and supported their policy with analysis, one of three criteria under policy recommendation, scored on a scale of 1 (insufficient), 2 (sufficient), or 3 (proficient).
Writing style had two criteria of style, flow, and concision as well as organization, scored on a scale of 1 (insufficient), 2 (sufficient), or 3 (proficient).
Organization, one of two criteria under writing style, scored on a scale of 1 (insufficient), 2 (sufficient), or 3 (proficient).

Students self-evaluated their professional English writing competency compared to other graduate students at CMU on a scale of 1 (worse than almost all graduate students at CMU) to 5 (better than almost all graduate students at CMU).
Students self-reported how useful the AI tool was for the summarization sub-task on a scale of 1 (not useful) to 4 (very useful).
Students self-reported how useful the AI tool was for the writing sub-task on a scale of 1 (not useful) to 4 (very useful).
Students self-reported how useful the AI tool was for the web research sub-task on a scale of 1 (not useful) to 4 (very useful).
Students self-reported how useful the AI tool was for the policy development sub-task on a scale of 1 (not useful) to 4 (very useful).
Students self-reported how useful the AI tool was for the analysis sub-task on a scale of 1 (not useful) to 4 (very useful).
Students self-reported how much they believe their research and writing speed improved from the first to the third assignment on a scale of 1 (did not improve) to 5 (significantly improved).
Students self-reported how much they believe their research and writing quality improved from the first to the third assignment on a scale of 1 (did not improve) to 5 (significantly improved).

Students self-reported how much they attribute any improvement in writing quality and/or speed to the use of AI versus experience gained in completing similar assignments on a scale of 1 (100% from completing similar assignments) to 10 (100% from using the AI tool), with 5 representing 50/50 improvement.

Students self-reported how much they value the benefits of AI tools to their writing quality versus speed on a scale of 1 (100% value quality) to 10 (100% value speed), with 5 representing equally value quality and speed improvement.

There was no main effect of English language status (f(1,23) = 1.09, p = .31, ηp2 = .05) meaning native English speakers and ESL students spent comparable time preparing their memo writing assignments.

01_extraction.notes: Study design: Mixed-methods study with 27 graduate students (one opted out of initial 28) at Carnegie Mellon University. Participants completed three writing assignments in fixed order: no genAI (Assignment 1), full genAI (Assignment 2, instructional tool only), and genAI-assisted (Assignment 3). Due to logistical and pedagogical constraints, the order of writing conditions was not counterbalanced. The paper notes this as a limitation for causal inference. Sample size limitations: The small sample size (n=27) limited statistical power to detect all possible effects and disaggregated analyses. Grading bias: The grading rubric was implemented by the professor who was not blinded to condition, though this was mitigated by a detailed rubric. Multiple comparisons: The reported p-values are uncorrected for multiple comparisons. Confounding: While significant effects on writing speed and quality were found, the paper acknowledges that other factors (e.g., repeated practice) could have partially contributed to improvements, though students attributed improvement more to genAI than practice. Sample characteristics: 85.2% of participants had prior experience using genAI for coursework, representing a self-selected group interested in an AI elective. Arm-level sample sizes (n) are not explicitly stated in the quotes for the no_genai and genai_assisted arms; the total sample was 27 participants, but the quotes do not specify how many completed each assignment. Differential effects: While not statistically significant, ESL students showed slightly greater improvements in both productivity and performance with genAI assistance, suggesting a potential equity benefit. Correlation analysis: Perceived quality improvement significantly correlated with actual grade change (r=.45, p=.02, R²=.20).

02_method.rationale: The study states 'All participants completed three writing assignments in the order of no genAI, full genAI, and genAI-assisted' with the same subjects experiencing all conditions. Although 'the order of writing conditions was not counterbalanced,' this is still a within-subject design where participants serve as their own controls across conditions.

per_outcome.operationalization: Self-reported minutes actively engaged in completing memo writing assignments (research and writing combined)

Single rubric criterion scored 1 (insufficient), 2 (sufficient), or 3 (proficient)
Combined score on two rubric criteria (quality of research and integration/analysis), each scored 1-3
Single rubric criterion scored 1 (insufficient), 2 (sufficient), or 3 (proficient)

Combined score on two rubric criteria (style/flow/concision and organization), each scored 1-3
Single rubric criterion scored 1 (insufficient), 2 (sufficient), or 3 (proficient)
Self-evaluation of professional writing competency relative to CMU graduate students, scale 1-5. This is a self-perception measure, not an objective quality assessment of task output.

04_onet.rationale: The participants performed a complex sequence of professional research and writing tasks: 'summarization of a news article, web research to find survey results, analysis and synthesis of different survey results, policy development, and critical analysis.' These are graduate students practicing professional memo writing that requires gathering, organizing, analyzing, and synthesizing information from multiple sources to produce policy recommendations. This directly aligns with Processing Information (WA-14): compiling, coding, categorizing, calculating, tabulating, auditing, or verifying information or data. While the output is written communication, the core cognitive work being measured is information processing across research, analysis, and synthesis tasks.

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: Only one AI-assisted arm available. This is a within-subject design where all participants completed both a no-genAI assignment (first) and a genAI-assisted assignment (third).
llm_extracted.notes: Within-subject design with paired observations. The test statistic is reported as F(1,23) = 23.15, suggesting 24 total participants who each completed both conditions. Sample sizes (n) are not explicitly stated in the arms array but can be inferred from the F-test degrees of freedom. The mean difference is M_ai - M_human = 68.1 - 191.8 = -123.7 minutes. The reported percent change of 64.5% reduction is expressed as -0.645.
source_quotes: There was a significant main effect of writing condition on writing time (f(1,23) = 23.15, p < .001, ηp2 = .50) showing that students spent significantly less time writing their memo when they used genAI assistance.
On average, genAI assistance reduced the time spent completing their policy memo assignment by 64.5%.
First assignment completed without genAI assistance.
Third writing assignment completed with genAI assistance.
Every student chose to use genAI as an assistant.

computed.notes: Within-subject design with paired observations. The test statistic is reported as F(1,23) = 23.15, suggesting 24 total participants who each completed both conditions. Sample sizes (n) are not explicitly stated in the arms array but can be inferred from the F-test degrees of freedom. The mean difference is M_ai - M_human = 68.1 - 191.8 = -123.7 minutes. The reported percent change of 64.5% reduction is expressed as -0.645. | SDs/SEs missing; variance not computable

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: Only one AI arm (genai_assisted) compared to the human-only baseline (no_genai). This is a within-subjects design where the same students completed both assignments.
llm_extracted.notes: Within-subjects design with repeated measures ANOVA. Sample size n=26 inferred from df2=25 (n-1). The F-statistic is from a repeated-measures design. Mean difference = 94.1 - 88.3 = 5.8. The paper reports η²=.16 (eta-squared), not Cohen's d or Hedges' g. SD of difference scores not reported, but means, SDs, and F-statistic are available for paired-design Hedges' g calculation.
source_quotes: There was a significant main effect of writing condition (f(1,25) = 4.74, p = .04, ηp2 = .16) indicating that students earned higher grades on the assignment prepared with genAI assistance.
On average, students' grades change from a B+ (M = 88.3%, SD = 10.3%) without genAI to an A (M = 94.1%, SD = 8.0%) with genAI assistance.
First assignment completed without genAI assistance... Third writing assignment completed with genAI assistance.

computed.notes: Within-subjects design with repeated measures ANOVA. Sample size n=26 inferred from df2=25 (n-1). The F-statistic is from a repeated-measures design. Mean difference = 94.1 - 88.3 = 5.8. The paper reports η²=.16 (eta-squared), not Cohen's d or Hedges' g. SD of difference scores not reported, but means, SDs, and F-statistic are available for paired-design Hedges' g calculation.
